posts.title: How to Get a Baby to Sleep Faster
posts.excerpt: Five small changes that quietly cut bedtime in half — without a strict program or any tears.
posts.author: Peaceful Nights Guide
posts.content:
If putting your baby down takes an hour every night, you are not alone — and the fix is usually smaller than parents expect. Here are five changes that move the needle without forcing anything.

1. Move bedtime earlier, not later

Overtired babies fight sleep harder. Try moving bedtime fifteen to thirty minutes earlier for three nights and notice the difference.

2. Dim the lights an hour before

Bright light suppresses melatonin. Lamps and warm light help signal that the day is winding down.

3. Keep the routine boring

Bath, pyjamas, feed, story, bed — every night, same order. Predictability is calming for small nervous systems.

4. Watch for wake windows

Babies sleep best when they go down before they are overtired. Learn your baby's window and aim a few minutes inside it.

5. Stop adding new tricks

If a routine is mostly working, give it a week before changing anything. Consistency builds the habit.

If you want the full sequence laid out by age, that is exactly what the Peaceful Nights Sleep Guide covers.

posts.title: Bedtime Routine Checklist
posts.excerpt: A printable, step-by-step list you can stick on the fridge — so bedtime runs itself even on hard days.
posts.author: Peaceful Nights Guide
posts.content:
A routine works because it is predictable. Use this checklist as a starting point and adjust the order to fit your home.

One hour before bed

  • Dim the main lights
  • Quiet down the volume in the home
  • Last small snack or feed

Thirty minutes before bed

  • Warm bath (three to five minutes is enough)
  • Pyjamas and nappy
  • Brush teeth (or wipe gums)

Ten minutes before bed

  • One short book in the bedroom
  • A short lullaby or calming song
  • Final feed or water

Bedtime

  • Sleep sack or blanket
  • White noise on
  • Goodnight phrase — the same one every night
  • Out of the room while baby is calm but awake

Print this. Stick it where you can see it. On a hard day, you will not have to think — you will just follow the list.

posts.title: Common Toddler Sleep Mistakes (and how to fix them)
posts.excerpt: The four toddler bedtime habits that almost always backfire — and the gentle fix for each.
posts.author: Peaceful Nights Guide
posts.content:
Toddlers are not babies, and they are not small adults. They are negotiators with limited words and a lot of feelings. These four habits make bedtime harder than it needs to be.

1. Letting bedtime drift later

Toddlers seem to have endless energy at night, but late bedtimes usually cause more wake-ups, not fewer. Hold the line on a consistent time.

2. Negotiating from the doorway

Once you start saying yes from the doorway, the doorway becomes the negotiation zone. Stay calm, stay short, and exit.

3. Skipping the wind-down

Running, tickling, or screens right before bed wake the brain back up. Quiet activities only in the last thirty minutes.

4. Trying three new things at once

If something is not working, change one thing and give it a week. Parents who change everything every night never know what actually helped.

The full toddler section of the Peaceful Nights Sleep Guide has scripts you can use word-for-word at the doorway.
